scenario_name: Scenario 11 — Queue Backpressure and Consumer Lag
justification: All pass criteria met: throughput gap calculated (300/min vs 15-20/min with ~280/min growth rate), unbounded backlog consequence described (16,800 unprocessed jobs/hour), visibility timeout double-processing risk identified as mathematical certainty (10×3s=30s=timeout), missing DLQ identified as blocker, queue depth metric gap called out as critical, autoscaling to 15-20 workers recommended, verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptional throughput analysis — not only identified the gap but quantified backlog growth rate (280 jobs/min) and hourly accumulation (16,800 jobs)
Visibility timeout analysis goes beyond identification to explain WHY it's a mathematical certainty, not an edge case
DLQ absence correctly framed as a blocker with concrete recommendation (maxReceiveCount 3-5)
Queue depth metric gap called out as CRITICAL with excellent on-call reasoning — explains what the engineer cannot distinguish with only two counters
Goes well beyond rubric requirements: identifies idempotency gap, upstream failure handling, graceful shutdown, circuit breakers, and rollback considerations
Escalations are well-structured with clear routing and reasoning
Confidence section is honest about assumptions and low-confidence areas

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: throughput gap calculated (300/min vs 15-20/min with ~280/min growth rate), unbounded backlog consequence described (16,800 jobs after 1-hour peak, 14 hours to drain), visibility timeout double-processing risk identified with precise math (10×4s=40s > 30s timeout), missing DLQ identified as a blocker with poison message infinite-retry consequence, queue depth metric gap explicitly called out as the most important missing signal, autoscaling recommended (15-20 instances), verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally precise capacity math: not just identifying the gap but quantifying backlog accumulation rate (~280/min) and drain time (14 hours for 1-hour peak)
Visibility timeout analysis goes beyond identification — proves it's deterministic, not theoretical, with batch timing math
DLQ framed correctly as operational baseline, not a nice-to-have, with clear explanation of poison message capacity starvation
Outstanding observability critique: identifies that /health endpoint actively masks the problem by returning 200 while the system drowns
Goes beyond minimum rubric with idempotency concerns, circuit breaker gaps, graceful degradation, and deployment safety
Confidence section is well-calibrated — high confidence on deterministic math, low confidence on areas requiring code review

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: throughput gap clearly identified (300/min vs 15-20/min with 16,800 jobs/hour deficit), unbounded backlog consequence described, visibility timeout double-processing risk identified with precise arithmetic (10×3s=30s matching timeout), missing DLQ identified as a blocker, queue depth metric gap called out as critical, autoscaling recommended (15-20 workers at peak), and verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptional throughput math — not only identified the 15x gap but quantified the hourly deficit at 16,800 jobs/hour
Visibility timeout analysis is precise: correctly identifies that 10×3s=30s exactly matches the timeout, making this a guaranteed race condition, not an edge case
DLQ absence framed correctly as a blocker that compounds the throughput deficit by consuming worker capacity on poison messages
Queue depth metric gap identified as critical with clear operational consequence — backlog invisible until customers complain
Provided three concrete remediation options for the visibility timeout issue (increase timeout, reduce batch size, extend per-message) with a clear recommendation
Went beyond requirements to identify downstream failure modes (circuit breakers, idempotency, graceful degradation) that strengthen the NOT READY verdict
Escalations are well-structured with clear routing and reasoning
